label: Implement Cybersecurity Risk-Management Measures
description: Take appropriate and proportionate technical, operational and organisational measures to manage the risks posed to the security of network and information systems used for the provision of services, and to prevent or minimise the impact of incidents on recipients and other services. Measures must be based on an all-hazards approach and cover at least: (a) policies on risk analysis and information system security; (b) incident handling; (c) business continuity (backup management, disaster recovery, crisis management); (d) supply chain security including security aspects of relationships with direct suppliers; (e) security in network and information systems acquisition, development and maintenance; (f) policies and procedures to assess effectiveness of cybersecurity risk-management measures; (g) basic cyber hygiene practices and cybersecurity training; (h) policies and procedures for cryptography and encryption; (i) human resources security, access control policies and asset management; (j) use of multi-factor authentication, secured voice/video/text communications, and secured emergency communications.

label: Management Body Must Approve Cybersecurity Risk-Management Measures
description: The management body of the entity must approve the cybersecurity risk-management measures taken pursuant to Article 21, and oversee implementation. Management body members are personally liable for infringements by the entity.

label: Management Body Members Must Complete Cybersecurity Training
description: Members of the management body must follow training and shall encourage the entities they manage to offer similar training on a regular basis to all employees, so as to gain sufficient knowledge and skills to identify risks and assess cybersecurity risk-management practices and their impact on the entity's services.

label: Submit Early Warning Within 24 Hours of Significant Incident
description: Without undue delay, and in any event within 24 hours of becoming aware of a significant incident, notify the CSIRT or competent authority with an early warning indicating whether the incident is suspected to be caused by unlawful or malicious acts and whether it could have a cross-border impact.

label: Submit Incident Notification Within 72 Hours
description: Without undue delay and in any event within 72 hours of becoming aware of the significant incident, submit an incident notification to the CSIRT or competent authority, updating the early warning information and providing an initial assessment of the significant incident (including its severity and impact) and, where available, indicators of compromise.

label: Submit Final Incident Report Within One Month
description: No later than one month after the submission of the incident notification, submit a final report to the CSIRT or competent authority including: (i) a detailed description of the incident (severity and impact); (ii) the type of threat or root cause likely to have triggered the incident; (iii) applied and ongoing mitigation measures; (iv) the cross-border impact of the incident where applicable.

label: Submit Intermediate Incident Progress Report on Request
description: Upon request by a CSIRT or competent authority, submit an intermediate progress report on relevant status updates after submitting the incident notification.

label: Notify Service Recipients of Significant Cyber Threat
description: Where a significant cyber threat is identified that could adversely affect the provision of services, inform recipients who may be affected by that threat of any measures or remedies they can take in response. Where appropriate, also notify the CSIRT or competent authority of the threat.

label: Register with Competent Authority
description: Essential and important entities must register with the competent authority by submitting at minimum: name, address and contact details including email, IP ranges, and sector and subsector. Notify any changes within 2 weeks. Member States may set up national registration mechanisms.

label: Submit Registration Information to ENISA Registry
description: DNS service providers, TLD name registries, cloud computing service providers, data centre service providers, content delivery network providers, managed service providers, managed security service providers, and providers of online marketplaces, online search engines and social networking services platforms must submit registration information to ENISA including: name, address, contact details, IP ranges, sector and subsector, relevant Member States, and the Member State of main establishment.

label: Maintain Accurate Domain Name Registration Database
description: TLD name registries and domain name registration service providers must collect and maintain accurate and complete domain name registration data in a dedicated database (WHOIS equivalent) with due diligence. The database must include data necessary to identify and contact registrants and points of contact managing domain names, including: name, email address, phone number of registrant and registrant's contact point.

label: Provide Access to Domain Registration Data Within 72 Hours
description: TLD name registries and domain name registration service providers must make non-personal domain registration data publicly available without undue delay. Upon receiving a lawful and duly justified request from a legitimate access seeker, grant access to specific domain name registration data without undue delay and in any event within 72 hours.

label: Designate a Representative in the Union
description: Entities referred to in Article 26(1)(b) that are not established in the Union but offer services within the Union (DNS service providers, TLD registries, cloud, data centre, CDN, managed service, MSSSP, online marketplace, search engine, social networking platforms) must designate in writing a representative established in the Union. The representative may be held liable for non-compliance.
